skill.name: running-claude-code-via-litellm-copilot
skill.description: Guides setup and troubleshooting for routing Claude Code through a local LiteLLM proxy backed by GitHub Copilot.
skill.summary: Configure Claude Code to use a local LiteLLM proxy with GitHub Copilot.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed files are Markdown guidance and examples rather than executable code. Most detections are false positives from inline commands, model names, URLs, and configuration keys, but the skill has legitimate operational risk because it guides proxy routing, package installation, and optional persistent Claude Code configuration.

medium_findings.title: Unofficial Proxy Routing Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally routes Claude Code requests through a local LiteLLM proxy and then to GitHub Copilot. This is not malicious, but it can affect code privacy, service compliance, and audit expectations because user prompts and code may leave the local Claude Code path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The routing behavior is explicit and central to the skill. The files also include compliance caveats, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Claude Code Configuration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes guidance for merging environment variables into `~/.claude/settings.json`. It warns not to overwrite the file, but persistent configuration can redirect future Claude Code sessions until the user changes it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden-file modification path is documented and guarded by merge-safety instructions. The risk remains meaningful because it changes future tool behavior.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Examples Require User Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many shell and PowerShell snippets. These are documentation examples for checking tools, installing LiteLLM, starting a local proxy, and setting environment variables, not commands executed automatically by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear inside Markdown code blocks or inline guidance. No executable script, hook, or automation file is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the reviewed locations contain descriptive text, model names, or configuration guidance. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation and do not implement cryptography. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Localhost Endpoints
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs. The external URL is a cited article source, and the localhost URLs are examples for a local proxy endpoint rather than an exfiltration destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL contexts are visible in documentation examples. No evidence found that the skill sends data to an unauthorized remote endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Contains Package Install and Proxy Startup Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends `uv tool install`, `pip install`, and `litellm --config` commands. These are normal setup commands, but marketplace users should understand they install software and start a network-listening local service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and user-facing. They are not hidden execution, but they can change the local environment when followed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Contains Persistent Hidden-File Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes writing environment settings into `~/.claude/settings.json`. The instructions include preservation safeguards, but the pattern still touches a hidden configuration file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file path and merge behavior are explicit in the guidance. The content warns against wholesale replacement, reducing but not eliminating risk.

content.value_statement: Claude Code users may want a local proxy path to GitHub Copilot, but setup details can be confusing. This skill gives structured setup, verification, and troubleshooting guidance for LiteLLM-backed Copilot routing.
actual_capabilities: Explains the Claude Code to LiteLLM to GitHub Copilot request path.
Provides minimal LiteLLM configuration using the GitHub Copilot provider pattern.
Shows temporary PowerShell, Bash, and Zsh environment setup for Claude Code.
Guides safe persistent configuration by merging selected keys into Claude settings.
Troubleshoots model mismatch, missing localhost traffic, and Copilot authorization failures.
limitations: Does not decide whether the workaround complies with current GitHub terms.
Does not guarantee Copilot model availability or long-term provider compatibility.
Requires the user to install and run LiteLLM locally.
Does not replace normal secret handling or enterprise approval processes.

use_cases.title: First-Time Local Proxy Setup
use_cases.description: Set up a temporary LiteLLM proxy so Claude Code can test a Copilot-backed model route.

use_cases.title: Team Environment Documentation
use_cases.description: Document repeatable setup steps, environment variables, and verification checks for internal developer machines.

use_cases.title: Proxy Failure Diagnosis
use_cases.description: Investigate model-not-found errors, missing localhost requests, and GitHub Copilot authentication failures.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Claude Code, LiteLLM, and GitHub Copilot fit together. Include the main risks and prerequisites.

prompt_templates.title: Create Temporary Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Give me temporary setup commands for my shell to run Claude Code through LiteLLM on port 4000.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot No Proxy Traffic
prompt_templates.prompt: Claude Code starts, but LiteLLM logs show no request. Walk me through checks for environment variables, port use, and shell session scope.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Persistent Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me merge persistent Claude Code environment settings without overwriting unrelated keys in my existing settings file.

output_examples.input: I want a temporary setup for Bash.
output_examples.output: The response gives a compliance caveat, a minimal LiteLLM model configuration, Bash environment exports, and a verification checklist.

output_examples.input: LiteLLM says the model does not exist.
output_examples.output: The response compares the Claude Code model value with the LiteLLM model name, checks spelling, and explains how to retest.

output_examples.input: Make this persistent in Claude Code settings.
output_examples.output: The response asks for confirmation, preserves unrelated settings, and describes only the environment keys that need to be merged.

best_practices: Start with temporary environment variables before changing persistent settings.
Keep the Claude Code model value and LiteLLM model name exactly identical.
Watch LiteLLM logs while sending a small Claude Code request.
anti_patterns: Do not claim GitHub officially supports this workaround.
Do not replace the entire Claude settings file for a small environment change.
Do not treat a dummy Anthropic token as a real Copilot credential.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and command examples that a user or agent may choose to run.

faq.question: Is this an official GitHub Copilot workflow?
faq.answer: No. The skill presents it as an advanced workaround and tells users to review current terms and limits.

faq.question: Why does Claude Code need an Anthropic token value?
faq.answer: Claude Code expects a non-empty value locally, but the placeholder is not the GitHub Copilot credential.

faq.question: What is the safest first setup?
faq.answer: Use temporary environment variables and a local LiteLLM config before making persistent changes.

faq.question: What should I check when no traffic reaches LiteLLM?
faq.answer: Check the base URL, the LiteLLM port, the active shell session, and local firewall or port conflicts.

faq.question: Can model availability change?
faq.answer: Yes. Copilot-exposed model availability can change, so users should verify the current model route.
